title: Our clients are saying No to Vibe Coding | Vibe Coding vs Manual Coding
transcript: We had some of our clients literally say that, "Do not use wipe coding. Use manual coding instead." While wipe coding is extremely powerful, it can boost the efficiency [music] 10x. There are cases where manual coding is useful. I run two different companies. Atlik, a team of 50. Code Basics, [music] a team of 25. And we have been using wipe coding in both of our companies for past few months. In this video, I want to share a difference that we have observed between wipe coding [music] and manual coding based on our real experience. Let's go ahead and talk to the CTO of Atlik, Pranav. Hey, Pranav. Hi. Majority of our clients in Atlik are small to medium-scale enterprises based in US and UAE. Out of all these clients, how many clients are specifically asking us not to use wipe coding? So, I would say around 40 to 50% still prefer manual coding. Okay, and that's kind of interesting. So, what is the reason behind it? The primary reason is their IP either on code algorithm. And [music] let's say if they have a unique idea. And one of the major reason is also data sensitivity. Okay, understood. When you're doing wipe coding, your product idea, your data uh even any algorithmic novelty or IP that you're bringing in your product is going to cloud server. It is going to GPT server. And they definitely don't want uh >> [music] >> to leak that information. Now, I can think about easy solution, which is you set up a local machine, you set up Ollama, and you run your models there, and you use it for wipe coding. So, why can't they do that? They can do [music] it. Absolutely, it's a very good option. But, uh I see two drawbacks over there. First of all, it is very costly to set up a server for a model including GPUs. And second thing is those models are not as good as uh GPT or Opus. Understood. >> using Quant or Mistral, it is not going to provide that quality of code. Okay, [music] that's interesting. In terms of benefits and drawbacks that you have seen for wipe coding, like do you want to mention few of them? Yeah, sure. Uh I will start with benefits. So, first of all, productivity has increased a lot. What I was used to build in a year with four to five developers, now I alone can build it in couple of months. If I tell you about drawbacks, like because of token utilization, I'm working more [music] now. Mhm. And another thing is I still see uh issues with certain technologies like let's say mobile application or certain languages that it is breaking another module if I'm working on some of the related modules. Okay. So, you're trying to fix something and it will break something else. Yes. Okay, that's interesting. Also, I want to mention something interesting that we are noticing on the popular freelancing platform Upwork. On Upwork, nowadays we are seeing many jobs where people have wipe coded an app and they have created this mess, and now they need programmer's help to fix those. So, let's look at those jobs. Okay, so this is the first job that we are going to look at it. And here the person is saying that I have been wipe coded app, whatever. It generates this thing. And the app mostly works, but we are hitting production blocking bugs and inconsistencies. Now, using Claude code and AI tools, they are not able to fix these inconsistencies. And they are hiring experienced programmers. So, the experienced programmers will do manual coding. So, this is one place where manual coding will have a value. Look at this other job. The app is already built, but currently experiencing issues with functionality, integration, and performance. Okay. So, this is where manual coding will shine, where you have coded something using Claude code or AI tool. Now, you want to scale it. You want to fix an issue which AI cannot fix. You want to deploy things to production. At that time, you need seasoned programmers and manual coding. The discussion that we just had now with Pranav was our perspective on wipe coding uh in our company Atlik, which is a small company. But, when it comes to big companies like Amazon, you hear all these news stories where they had an outage because of AI-assisted coding. Okay, and you are hearing many such news in social media all the time. I want to share another incident where our AI engineer was building a rag pipeline, and Claude produced this particular code. They were using Docile. And apparently, this code was not parsing things properly, you know? It was not giving us this kind of nice markdown for a hierarchy. So, he went to GitHub issues for that Docile library, and he found a known issue, and he scanned through all the discussions, and there was a workaround. And the workaround was to use this result processor. Okay. So, later on, they added this code. Now, with Claude, after some iteration, it was not able to figure it out. So, now at this time, you need engineering skills. You need strong fundamentals. So, that you understand what is going on, why is it failing. And you need that attitude that you can go to GitHub. You need to know, okay, how to scan through issues. You need to know how to apply workaround. You can't apply a random workaround. You need to have a judgment and knowledge. Okay, that was one of the incidents where we thought wipe coding is not working, and manual coding, strong fundamentals, engineering skills are required. Now, obviously, there are many success stories that we have seen with wipe coding. One example is this company every.to, where they have written 95% of their code using AI. Okay. So, there was this video that I was watching, so I thought I would uh bring this up. This is one of those AI-native companies. In our company Code Basics, we ran a 45-day builder sprint, where even non-technical people built real SaaS products. Okay, for our own usage. For example, our marketing manager, her name is Padmini, she created a tool called Sigma, not Figma, Sigma, which can help her create the visuals and the necessary assets to run her PPC ads. Okay. Now, this is not just Gemini, okay? It is something on top of it, where you can add all your parameters, uh different configuration that you need to set for your ad, and it will create a nice-looking visual. Our operations manager generated a tool for uh mock interviews. Then we had our AI team member who generated practice platform, where you can practice Python problems, where you can practice ML problems, and so on. And I myself created a mobile app for Code Basics in 4 days. I don't know mobile app development, by the way. And if I had a mobile app developer working on this, they would have taken at least 6 months. So, we are seeing this kind of enormous productivity gains. But, at the end, to deploy all the things that we built during this 45-day sprint, we had to take the help of our engineering team. Because these non-technical folks do not know how to deploy things in production, how to set up scaling, monitoring, cost control. They don't know many things, actually. So, you can't just go ahead and blindly deploy it, otherwise it can make errors, you know? If you think about sensitive industries like health care and finance, a single mistake can cost you so much. Okay. Uh to summarize, based on our experience in our two companies, our view is that as a human, your role is that of an orchestrator. Then you use all these tools, okay? Claude code, Cursor. You are deploying things in AWS. You're using monitoring platform like DataDog, etc. You need to orchestrate things. So, you can do it really fast using AI, but you can't be blind and ignorant. You need to have strong fundamentals. You need to understand, you know, what is observability, what is monitoring, how do you do your DataDog setup. How do you set things up in cloud, etc. Maybe the models will improve in the future, where you just give it a command and it will take care of everything. Uh but right now, we are in this stage where, at least what we are feeling based on our experience, is that the human intervention is needed. You can, of course, build things really fast, but when it makes mistakes, when it comes to deployment, when it comes to scaling, all of these scenarios, the manual coding, the knowledge of engineering, the knowledge of strong fundamentals is extremely crucial. Folks, in the future, of course, AI models will improve, and they will be able to do things which they are not able to do today, but that is future. I just wanted to mention the today's current state based on our experience. Maybe somebody working for some big companies or working in a different environment will have a different perspective. We are not judging. We just wanted to share our honest opinion. Uh if you have any questions, please post in the comment box below. Uh I'll be happy to discuss it out. Thank you very much for watching. >> [music]
